article_title: 5 networking tools I use weekly that don't cost anything
core_thesis: The article argues that effective home networking does not require expensive enterprise software; instead, a curated set of free, lightweight tools can solve specific networking problems—such as remote access, traffic monitoring, speed testing, and outbound firewall control—without adding complexity or cost. The author shares five personally vetted tools that have become essential for managing a home lab and self-hosted services.
detailed_summary: The author, Shekhar Vaidya, presents five free networking tools he uses weekly to manage his home network and self-hosted services. Termius serves as a unified terminal for SSH access to local and remote servers, storing hosts and credentials for one-click connections. Tailscale provides secure remote access without port forwarding or static IPs, even working behind CGNAT, and requires minimal maintenance after setup. Sniffnet is a lightweight network monitoring tool that gives real-time visibility into traffic and bandwidth usage, useful for quick troubleshooting without complex packet analysis. OpenSpeedTest is a self-hosted speed test that measures local network throughput, unaffected by public server congestion, ideal for validating LAN performance. Simplewall offers fine-grained outbound firewall control on Windows, working alongside the built-in firewall to block or allow application-level connections with minimal complexity. The author emphasizes that each tool solves a specific problem and is lightweight, practical, and free.

entities.name: Shekhar Vaidya
entities.role: Author and technology journalist, computing writer at XDA

entities.name: Termius
entities.role: Unified terminal and SSH client for local and remote server access

entities.name: Tailscale
entities.role: VPN and remote access tool that works without port forwarding, even behind CGNAT

entities.name: Sniffnet
entities.role: Lightweight network monitoring tool for real-time traffic visibility

entities.name: OpenSpeedTest
entities.role: Self-hosted speed test for measuring local network throughput

entities.name: Simplewall
entities.role: Windows firewall controller for outbound application-level control

key_data_points: Termius stores SSH hosts, keys, and credentials for one-click connections, enabling seamless switching between local development and self-hosted environments (🟢 relevant to your DevOps/homelab: simplifies managing multiple servers and projects).

Tailscale provides secure remote access without port forwarding or static IPs, and works behind CGNAT—a common ISP limitation (🟢 relevant to your homelab: enables access to self-hosted services from anywhere without exposing them to the public internet).

Sniffnet offers real-time network traffic visibility with minimal setup—just select the network adapter—making it ideal for quick troubleshooting of unfamiliar traffic or bandwidth spikes (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to detect anomalous outbound connections or suspicious activity on your network).

OpenSpeedTest is self-hosted, so results are not affected by public server congestion; it measures real LAN throughput between devices (🟢 relevant to your homelab: helps validate network performance and isolate issues to internal vs. external connectivity).

Simplewall provides per-application outbound firewall control on Windows without replacing the built-in firewall, useful for blocking unwanted connections from unfamiliar software (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to monitor and restrict outbound traffic from potentially malicious applications).
methodology_steps: Install Termius: Download and install Termius on your system. Add your SSH hosts, keys, and credentials to create a centralized terminal hub for local and remote servers.

Set up Tailscale: Install Tailscale on all devices you want to connect. Sign in to the same Tailscale account on each device. No port forwarding or firewall changes needed; devices automatically discover each other and establish encrypted connections.

Use Sniffnet for quick monitoring: Download and run Sniffnet. Select the appropriate network adapter (e.g., Wi-Fi or LAN) from the list. Observe real-time traffic and bandwidth usage to identify unusual activity.

Deploy OpenSpeedTest: Host OpenSpeedTest on a local server or machine (e.g., via Docker or direct installation). Access the web interface from other devices on the same network to run speed tests and measure LAN throughput.

Configure Simplewall: Install Simplewall on Windows. It will list all applications attempting outbound connections. Use the interface to block or allow specific apps as needed, working alongside the default Windows Firewall.
conclusions: The five tools—Termius, Tailscale, Sniffnet, OpenSpeedTest, and Simplewall—form a practical, free toolkit for home networking and self-hosting. Each addresses a specific need (remote access, monitoring, speed testing, firewall control) without bloat or cost, making them ideal for homelab enthusiasts and developers.

Tailscale is particularly valuable for users with CGNAT or dynamic IPs, as it eliminates the need for port forwarding and static IPs while maintaining security. Its low maintenance after setup makes it a set-and-forget solution for remote access.

Simplewall fills a gap in Windows' native firewall by providing per-application outbound control, which is useful for both security (blocking suspicious apps) and troubleshooting (identifying which apps are phoning home).
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly supports your homelab and DevOps interests by providing a curated set of free, lightweight networking tools that solve common problems like remote access (Tailscale), terminal management (Termius), network monitoring (Sniffnet), speed testing (OpenSpeedTest), and outbound firewall control (Simplewall). For your Red Teaming practice, Sniffnet can help detect anomalous traffic, and Simplewall can be used to control outbound connections from tools or malware samples. As a student, these tools are cost-effective and easy to set up, allowing you to build a professional-grade home network without spending money. The article also reinforces the value of self-hosted solutions, which aligns with your ambition to take initiative and learn by doing.
extra_advice: Consider combining Tailscale with Simplewall to create a secure remote access pipeline: use Tailscale for encrypted tunnels to your homelab, and Simplewall to restrict which local apps can use that tunnel. This adds a layer of control for Red Teaming exercises where you want to isolate certain traffic. Also, try deploying OpenSpeedTest in a Docker container to quickly benchmark your LAN performance before and after network changes.
